audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 5 draft aligned to ark in Ashdod, Dagon falls, and plague movement through Philistine cities; later source/witness audit required.
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebrew-first naming, restored/mystical separation, narrative ambiguity, institutional power, vulnerable persons, violence, and misuse risks.

verses.restored: Philistines took ark of God and brought it from Ebenezer to Ashdod.
Philistines took ark of God, brought it into house of Dagon, and set it beside Dagon.
Ashdodites rose early next day, and look, Dagon had fallen upon his face to ground before ark of YHWH. They took Dagon and returned him to his place.

They rose early in morning next day, and look, Dagon had fallen upon his face to ground before ark of YHWH. Head of Dagon and two palms of his hands were cut off upon threshold; only Dagon's trunk remained upon him.
Therefore priests of Dagon and all who enter house of Dagon do not tread upon threshold of Dagon in Ashdod to this day.
Hand of YHWH was heavy upon Ashdodites. He devastated them and struck them with tumors, Ashdod and its territories.
Men of Ashdod saw that it was so and said, Ark of God of Israel shall not remain with us, for his hand is hard upon us and upon Dagon our god.

They sent and gathered to themselves all lords of Philistines and said, What shall we do with ark of God of Israel? They said, Let ark of God of Israel turn around to Gath. So they turned ark of God of Israel around.
After they turned it around, hand of YHWH was against city with a very great panic. He struck people of city, from small to great, and tumors broke out upon them.
They sent ark of God to Ekron. As ark of God came to Ekron, Ekronites cried out, saying, They have turned ark of God of Israel around to me to put me and my people to death.

They sent and gathered all lords of Philistines and said, Send away ark of God of Israel, and let it return to its place, so it does not put me and my people to death. For there was a panic of death in all city; hand of God was very heavy there.
Humans who did not die were struck with tumors, and cry of city went up to heavens.

translation_notes: Dagon's falls are ancient polemical narrative. They may not justify vandalizing other religions' images, mocking worshipers, persecuting religious minorities, or claiming modern interfaith supremacy.
The word often translated 'tumors' may refer to swellings, possibly hemorrhoidal or plague-related, and is linked in chapter 6 with mice and land devastation. Medical certainty is not possible.
Illness and epidemic are not evidence that sufferers worship wrongly, are morally contaminated, or deserve pain.

The narrative attributes the outbreak to hand of YHWH. This ancient theological claim must not be used to blame communities for disease, reject medicine, or identify current disasters as Divine punishment.
Philistine cities deliberate collectively and protest transfer of danger. Their cries and desire to protect life are humanly intelligible, not villainous.
Moving the ark from Ashdod to Gath to Ekron shifts risk onto other populations. Leaders must not solve crises by exporting danger to less powerful communities.
Dagon's severed head and hands form symbolic dethronement, not a mandate for mutilation.
The cry of the city reaching heaven affirms that suffering outside Israel is heard; Philistines are not outside moral concern.
Language of 'small to great' signals social breadth, not equal vulnerability; epidemic effects often follow power and resource inequalities.
safety_note: Do not use Dagon's fall to justify religious vandalism or contempt for other faiths. Do not call disease Divine punishment, stigmatize tumors or disability, reject medicine, or export hazards to another community. Philistine suffering and protest matter; their cries are heard.
